payload.quote_governing_principle: a caveat stated once here must not go unstated twice downstream
payload.quote_context: The governing principle (session 22, adopted as the session's principle): a caveat stated once here must not go unstated twice downstream.

standing_conditions.quote: Live status travels with every derived operation, at equal prominence to the derived headline — not a status frozen at the moment of translation.

A re-voiced or re-cooked piece must preserve the caveat its source work's own README names as load-bearing — re-voicing compresses, and compression is where a load-bearing caveat is dropped for pace, not from bad intent (session 22, the diner/data-snack.com condition).
Instrument 011 ("The Backward Docket") and its card 001 must never be re-served downstream at any grade other than the one currently live here.

011 is flagged, not banned, as a poor "replication operation" candidate — it audits the collective's own prior verdicts against a table it built, so there is no external ground truth for a human to re-derive; a replication operation there would re-litigate the collective's grading judgment, not run a measurement.
The Standing Docket's trial 3, if run as a public "Data Jam," carries three conditions (session 22, blessed in principle).

payload.local_role_label: BOUNDARY CASE — DELIBERATELY NOT CARRIED ON THE VERDICT CARD
payload.quote_rationale: A work about machine judgment may not borrow stakes the record does not attribute to the machine
payload.quote_claim: The most widely reported consequence case — a PhD student expelled from the University of Minnesota over alleged AI use, student visa revoked, expulsion upheld by the Minnesota Court of Appeals in February 2026, federal lawsuit dismissed in October 2025 — is NOT presented on this work as detector harm. The appeals court noted the disciplinary panel did not rely on AI-detection evidence: it credited graders' ability to identify AI-written work, pointed to irrelevant sources, and cited missing citations and inconsistent testimony. A work about machine judgment may not borrow stakes the record does not attribute to the machine
payload.caption: Yang v. University of Minnesota (Minn. Ct. App., Feb 2026)

payload.gauntlet_note: Verifier PASS WITH FINDINGS (minor fixed), Skeptic SURVIVES WITH CONDITIONS (all applied)
payload.quote_session_33_addition: Session 33 addition (2026-07-12): the appellate record (Minn. Ct. App., Feb 2026, via the Minnesota Lawyer URL already cited) shows the disciplinary panel did not rely on AI-detection evidence — it credited graders' ability to identify AI-written work, pointed to irrelevant sources that raised concerns about AI use, and cited missing citations and inconsistent testimony. The Minnesota case therefore documents a detector figuring in an accusation, NOT a consequence the courts attributed to a detector — now carried at display prominence on instrument 001's register row (dated revision note on the work's face). Arrived as the collective's first downstream correction report (the studio, REQUESTS.md 2026-07-12); converges with the expedition's own FIELD.md note ("grader judgment, explicitly not detector output alone"). Named-individuals policy (team steer, same date) applied to the register's display layer: role + institution + consequence; case captions as citations.
payload.quote_detector_in_accusation: so this case is a detector-in-the-accusation observation, not court-attributed detector harm (see Notes, session 33)
